name: AMITRIPTYLINE
dosage: Avoid; if necessary reduce to 25% of standard dose with TDM
mechanism: CYP2D6*4 (rs3892097) is a null allele; *10 (rs1065852) confers severely reduced enzyme activity. Combined PM phenotype abolishes hepatic N-demethylation and hydroxylation of amitriptyline, causing 4–10x plasma accumulation.
whyRisk: Supraphysiologic plasma concentrations cause QTc prolongation, anticholinergic toxicity, and serotonin syndrome risk.

name: NORTRIPTYLINE
dosage: Avoid; if required, initiate at 25% of normal dose with therapeutic drug monitoring
mechanism: Nortriptyline is the active metabolite of amitriptyline and is itself hydroxylated by CYP2D6. PM phenotype (*4/*10) severely impairs 10-hydroxylation, causing toxic accumulation.
whyRisk: Plasma nortriptyline above 150 ng/mL is associated with cardiac arrhythmia, seizure, and anticholinergic crisis.

name: ATOMOXETINE
dosage: Initiate at 0.5 mg/kg/day; titrate slowly; do not exceed 80 mg/day
mechanism: CYP2D6 is the primary route of atomoxetine metabolism to 4-hydroxyatomoxetine. PM diplotype (*4/*10) results in up to 10-fold higher AUC versus extensive metabolizers.
whyRisk: Excessive exposure causes hypertension, tachycardia, severe insomnia, appetite suppression, and suicidal ideation risk in pediatric patients.

name: CODEINE
alternative: Morphine or oxycodone at standard doses with monitoring
mechanism: Codeine requires CYP2D6-mediated O-demethylation for conversion to morphine (active opioid). In PM phenotype (*4/*10), <1% conversion occurs, eliminating analgesic benefit while parent codeine accumulates.
whyRisk: No analgesic efficacy. Accumulated unconverted codeine may cause nausea, constipation, and dysphoria without pain relief. FDA Black Box Warning applies.

name: TRAMADOL
mechanism: Tramadol is a prodrug requiring CYP2D6-mediated O-demethylation to O-desmethyltramadol (M1), the mu-opioid receptor agonist responsible for analgesia. PM phenotype abolishes this conversion.
whyRisk: PM patients derive no meaningful analgesia. Risk of serotonergic toxicity from accumulation of parent compound acting on serotonin reuptake inhibition.

name: METOPROLOL
dosage: Reduce initial dose by 50%; titrate based on heart rate and blood pressure response
mechanism: Metoprolol undergoes extensive CYP2D6-mediated alpha-hydroxylation and O-demethylation. PM phenotype (*4/*10) causes 4–5x higher steady-state plasma concentrations.
whyRisk: Excessive beta-blockade causes symptomatic bradycardia, hypotension, fatigue, and atrioventricular block, particularly at standard doses.

name: PAROXETINE
dosage: Reduce dose; consider 50% of standard dose with clinical monitoring
mechanism: Paroxetine is both a substrate and potent inhibitor of CYP2D6. In PM phenotype (*4/*10), reduced metabolism leads to significant plasma accumulation, further inhibiting residual CYP2D6 activity.
whyRisk: Elevated paroxetine concentrations increase risk of serotonin syndrome, QTc prolongation, and discontinuation syndrome severity.

name: HALOPERIDOL
dosage: Reduce dose by 25–50%; monitor for extrapyramidal symptoms
mechanism: CYP2D6 is the primary enzyme for haloperidol oxidative metabolism. PM phenotype accumulates haloperidol and its reduced metabolite, increasing dopaminergic blockade.
whyRisk: Higher plasma levels increase risk of tardive dyskinesia, prolonged QTc, and neuroleptic malignant syndrome.

name: CITALOPRAM
dosage: Maximum 20 mg/day; monitor ECG for QTc prolongation
mechanism: CYP2C19*2 (rs4244285) and *3 (rs4986893) are both loss-of-function alleles. Compound PM genotype (*2/*3) eliminates CYP2C19-mediated N-demethylation of citalopram, causing 2–3x plasma accumulation at standard doses.
whyRisk: Supratherapeutic citalopram concentrations are associated with dose-dependent QTc prolongation and Torsades de Pointes. FDA limits dose to 40 mg/day in general population; CPIC recommends 20 mg/day max for PM.

name: ESCITALOPRAM
dosage: Reduce to 50% of standard dose; maximum 10 mg/day
alternative: Sertraline or fluvoxamine with dose adjustment
mechanism: Escitalopram (S-enantiomer of citalopram) is primarily metabolized by CYP2C19 via S-demethylation. PM diplotype (*2/*3) causes significant drug accumulation versus extensive metabolizers.
whyRisk: Increased escitalopram exposure causes QTc prolongation and heightened risk of serotonin-related adverse effects.

name: CLOPIDOGREL
alternative: Prasugrel 10 mg/day or Ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily (non-CYP2C19 prodrugs)
mechanism: Clopidogrel requires two-step CYP2C19-mediated oxidation to generate the active thiol metabolite that irreversibly inhibits P2Y12. Both *2 (rs4244285) and *3 (rs4986893) are null alleles, completely abolishing bioactivation in PM diplotype.
whyRisk: PM patients on clopidogrel show >90% reduction in active metabolite exposure and near-zero P2Y12 inhibition, conferring a 2–3.5x higher risk of MACE, stent thrombosis, and recurrent MI. FDA Black Box Warning mandates CYP2C19 testing.

name: VORICONAZOLE
dosage: Reduce maintenance dose by 50%; initiate TDM immediately; target trough 1–5.5 mg/L
mechanism: Voriconazole is predominantly metabolized by CYP2C19 (primary), CYP2C9, and CYP3A4. PM diplotype (*2/*3) results in 4–5x higher AUC relative to extensive metabolizers, with nonlinear PK amplification at standard doses.
whyRisk: Excessive voriconazole exposure causes visual disturbances, hepatotoxicity (ALT/AST elevation), photosensitivity, QTc prolongation, neurotoxicity, and periostitis with long-term use.

name: SERTRALINE
dosage: Consider 50% dose reduction; monitor clinically at standard dose initiation
alternative: Fluvoxamine with dose adjustment or mirtazapine
mechanism: Sertraline undergoes N-demethylation via CYP2C19 (major) and CYP2D6 (minor). PM diplotype (*2/*3) moderately increases sertraline exposure; however, clinical impact is less severe than citalopram due to flatter dose-response curve.
whyRisk: Mild to moderate plasma accumulation may increase GI adverse effects, insomnia, sexual dysfunction, and serotonin syndrome risk when combined with other serotonergic agents.

name: DIAZEPAM
dosage: Reduce dose and extend dosing interval; avoid prolonged use
mechanism: Diazepam is metabolized by CYP2C19 to active desmethyldiazepam (nordiazepam), and further by CYP3A4. PM diplotype (*2/*3) significantly reduces initial N-demethylation, prolonging diazepam half-life and accumulating active metabolites.
whyRisk: Prolonged sedation, respiratory depression, psychomotor impairment, and falls risk, particularly in elderly populations. Risk compounded with CNS depressants.
